responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a structural sovereignty and resilience crisis: MitID, the nation's critical digital identity layer, depends on Apple and Google not merely for distribution but for core trust signals and device integrity verification. This creates supplier concentration risk, contingency exposure, and conflicts with the 2026–2029 digital strategy mandate. The project's core tension is between technical feasibility and political/institutional inertia: building production MitID replacement is unrealistic, but influencing AltID procurement, EU standards, and fallback-path requirements is tractable if the project combines credible technical evidence, resilience-focused advocacy, and coalition leverage before the identity stack locks in the same dependencies. The selected levers target the critical intervention points—demonstrator credibility, procurement language, standards alignment, institutional buy-in, and continuity-of-service framing—that determine whether the project shifts from technical audit to policy and standards impact.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope and Trust Model
levers.consequences: A narrow, standards-based demonstrator (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS plus browser-based fallback) delivers credible evidence that platform-neutral authentication is technically feasible without requiring retrofits to MitID or claiming production readiness. This evidence becomes the foundation for procurement language and standards proposals in phases two and three. However, demonstrators perceived as toy implementations or oversimplified will be dismissed by incumbent operators and procurement officers; the project must balance lean scope against sufficient architectural depth to withstand expert skepticism.
options: Build a single integrated mobile-plus-browser demonstrator using OpenID Connect and FIDO2 against a full mock Danish identity-provider stack, with explicit trust-path documentation and threat modeling to support procurement arguments

Develop two independent demonstrators—one mobile-focused on GrapheneOS with platform-integrity alternatives clearly named, one browser-token-based—and publish a side-by-side trust model comparison that allows procurement to choose preferred fallback patterns

Create a minimal hardware-token-plus-web demonstrator that deliberately excludes mobile OS entirely, framing it as the most radical but lowest-risk fallback path for continuity planning and positioning the other options as incremental improvements
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the credibility-versus-scope trade-off, but the options underestimate the institutional cost of demonstrator governance. None address how the project will prevent the demonstrator from becoming a product roadmap pressure point or a target for incumbent veto based on 'incomplete security analysis.' Procurement officers will demand formal security review; the options do not allocate budget or timeline for external validation.

levers.name: Procurement Language and AltID Engagement
levers.consequences: Securing explicit platform-neutrality, fallback access, or supplier-concentration-risk language in official AltID design documents or procurement terms would lock in resilience requirements before architectural decisions harden. Success depends on formal institutional traction with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the AltID operator by mid-project. However, incumbent operators and platform vendors will resist language perceived as anti-commercial or technically prescriptive; weak institutional relationships or late-stage engagement will leave phase three with no realistic channel for procurement influence.
options: Target Digitaliseringsstyrelsen directly with a formal fallback-access requirement proposal in phase one, position the demonstrators as compliance evidence in phase three, and prepare a public procurement template that procurement officers can adopt without citing the project by name

Engage the MitID operator and AltID program leadership incrementally throughout phases one and two via technical working groups and security reviews, building consensus on resilience language before formal procurement starts, rather than attempting to inject requirements retroactively in phase three

Coordinate with the European Commission's eIDAS2 implementation task force to align fallback-access and platform-neutrality requirements at the EU level first, then use EU framework obligations to pressure Danish procurement to adopt parallel language, reducing reliance on bilateral Danish negotiation
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between direct Danish procurement influence and indirect EU-level leverage, but the options overlook timing risk. If AltID procurement accelerates faster than the project's EU engagement matures, Danish intervention becomes obsolete. None of the options provide a clear contingency if institutional relationships fail to develop or if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen deprioritizes the resilience framing in favor of user-convenience or cost arguments.

levers.name: Coalition Structure and Advocacy Framing
levers.consequences: Building a coalition spanning civil-society, academic, security, and parliamentary stakeholders increases political cover and channels for influence beyond formal procurement channels. Framing the issue as resilience and supplier concentration rather than ideology or privacy maximizes institutional receptiveness. However, coalition members often have divergent interests—privacy advocates may prioritize data minimization over fallback access, security experts may prioritize certification complexity over platform neutrality—and managing coalition messaging without alienating members is difficult; an internally fractured coalition can damage credibility more than no coalition at all.
options: Build a tight technical-policy coalition of 3–5 core institutions (Danish cybersecurity authority, parliament's digital committee representatives, one major university) with written joint position papers, avoiding broader civil-society partners whose agendas may conflict or dilute the resilience message

Construct a broad multi-stakeholder coalition (civil society, business, academic, parliamentary) with explicitly segmented messaging—resilience and procurement for government stakeholders, privacy and sovereignty for civil society, interoperability and risk mitigation for business and security experts—and maintain a public unified statement on supplier concentration risk

Prioritize parliamentary engagement via Folketinget's digital policy committee as the primary coalition anchor, treating other stakeholders as supporting voices rather than equal partners, and frame resilience requirements as a non-partisan continuity-planning obligation that should not require civil-society consensus
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between coalition breadth and message coherence, but the options overlook the risk of captured messaging. If coalition partners include industry players or operators with commercial stakes in the status quo, coalition credibility becomes hostage to accusations of conflicts of interest. None of the options address how the project will maintain independence if coalition members demand editorial control over key deliverables or public statements.

levers.name: Standards Venue and EU Alignment
levers.consequences: Engaging with EU-level technical working groups and the European Digital Identity Wallet framework allows the project to amplify impact beyond Denmark and frame requirements as EU-wide interoperability concerns rather than national parochialism. Success here creates external pressure on Danish procurement to comply with EU standards. However, EU processes are slow and crowded; meaningful influence requires sustained engagement and technical expertise on standards bodies that the project may lack, and EU-level language may be weaker or more generic than Danish-specific procurement requirements.
options: Focus the project's EU engagement narrowly on the eIDAS2 technical working group responsible for wallet architecture, targeting specific fallback-access or platform-neutrality language in the implementing technical specifications, with phase three dedicated to translating EU language into Danish procurement terms

Position the project as a Dutch-Danish bilateral initiative aligned with broader European digital sovereignty concerns, leveraging existing EU policy conversations around GAIA-X and digital autonomy to make platform-neutral identity a European strategic priority rather than a single-nation issue

Maintain EU engagement as a secondary, publication-focused effort rather than a primary advocacy channel; publish findings in open EU forums and standards consortia, allowing policy influence to propagate organically through networks rather than attempting to secure formal language commitments from EU institutions
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between EU-level leverage and Danish autonomy in procurement, but the options overlook the risk of misalignment. If EU standards evolve toward mandatory platform-neutral approaches, Denmark may be forced to overhaul AltID sooner than optimal; conversely, if EU action stalls, Danish advocacy becomes trapped in multilateral gridlock. None of the options address what the project does if EU bodies reject platform-neutrality language as anti-competitive or commercially hostile.

levers.name: Phase-Gate Funding Conditions and Risk Mitigation
levers.consequences: Conditional phase-gate funding (phase two and three dependent on phase one deliverables and institutional traction) protects budget against institutional failure but creates cash-flow and staffing continuity challenges if phase-gate conditions are not met or take longer to satisfy than anticipated. Clear phase-gate criteria increase accountability but may create pressure to manufacture deliverables that meet criteria on paper without genuine institutional engagement. Conversely, vague conditions allow flexibility but invite scope creep and budget hemorrhage.
options: Define phase-gate criteria tightly: phase two requires a published feasibility report, one formal meeting with a named authority, and a project financing plan; phase three requires documented institutional interest from AltID program leadership or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, or a documented contingency pivot toward publication and EU standards input instead of procurement targeting

Use phase gates as flexibility points rather than hard stops; define phase one as unconditional and phase two as conditional on 'meaningful institutional engagement' (defined as written correspondence or meeting documentation, not formal approval), allowing the project to continue phase two if phase-one outreach begins but has not yet yielded formal responses

Split phases two and three budget into demonstrator development (unconditional) and procurement intervention (conditional), so that technical work proceeds regardless of institutional traction, but procurement and standards effort is deferred or truncated if phase-one institutional engagement is weak
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between accountability and flexibility, but the options underestimate the political cost of visibly failing phase gates. If phase-one institutional engagement is weak and the project pivots to 'publication and EU standards input,' stakeholders may view the project as having failed its core mission regardless of technical quality. None of the options provide a clear mechanism for recovering credibility if early institutional outreach is rebuffed or if procurement timelines shift faster than the project can respond.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Positioning and Transparency Strategy
levers.consequences: The project does not attempt to build a production replacement for MitID or explicitly attack the MitID operator, but the project's framing of platform-vendor dependence as a risk necessarily implies that the operator's current trust model is problematic. The operator may interpret the project as a threat and attempt to block access to technical documentation, obstruct stakeholder relationships, or lobby against procurement changes. Transparency about the project's goals and technical assumptions can reduce adversarial escalation, but full transparency may accelerate incumbent opposition. Stealth or non-engagement invites accusations of bad faith if discovered later.
options: Approach the MitID operator early in phase one with a transparent but diplomatically framed proposal for a technical working relationship, positioning the project as supporting MitID's resilience and contingency preparedness rather than as a competitive threat, and requesting access to technical documentation and operator perspectives on fallback-access architecture

Treat the MitID operator as a stakeholder for consultation but not as a coalition partner; brief operator leadership on the project scope and findings but do not request approval or ongoing collaboration, maintaining independence from operator consent while avoiding public rhetoric that frames MitID's current model as fundamentally deficient

Conduct the project without formal outreach to the MitID operator until phase-three procurement intervention; document any operator objections or blocking attempts as evidence of incumbent resistance, and use public disclosure of barriers to platform-neutral requirements as a policy argument in favor of regulatory intervention
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between transparency and strategic autonomy, but the options overlook the asymmetry of information power. The MitID operator knows its own architecture and constraints; the project does not. Early operator engagement risks providing veto leverage to the incumbent, but non-engagement risks building demonstrators or proposals that the operator can quickly dismiss as technically infeasible. None of the options address how the project will verify operator claims about technical constraints or funding discontinuities if the operator's interests diverge from the project's goals.

levers.name: Fallback-Path Certification and Governance Model
levers.consequences: Any platform-neutral or fallback authentication path will eventually require formal certification, assurance review, or regulatory approval to be trusted in production MitID or successor contexts. The project's phase three must clarify whether fallback paths are certified within the existing MitID governance framework (conservative, slow, and operator-controlled) or within eIDAS2/NSIS frameworks (newer, less operator-dependent, but also less mature and with longer EU consensus timelines). Choosing the wrong governance path defers implementation indefinitely or locks fallback access into a framework that itself depends on platform vendors or foreign regulation.
options: Propose that fallback-access certification be integrated into the eIDAS2 Level Substantial assurance framework as a required 'resilience attestation,' allowing fallback paths to be certified independently of MitID governance and creating a clean separation between primary and backup identity paths

Develop a lightweight self-certification or voluntary-assurance model for fallback paths that does not require formal MitID-operator approval or EU-level certification, allowing demonstrators to be deployed in pilot or non-critical contexts without regulatory gatekeeping, and using real-world pilot data to inform formal certification requirements later

Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to establish a contingency-path certification fast-track within Danish digital identity governance, creating a parallel certification process for fallback authentication that is explicitly faster and lighter than MitID certification but still maintains sufficient assurance to meet eIDAS2 substantial-level requirements
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between certification rigor and deployment speed, but the options obscure a fundamental governance risk. If fallback paths are certified independently of MitID, they may not interoperate with production MitID systems in a crisis; if they are certified within MitID governance, the MitID operator retains veto power over fallback requirements. None of the options address whether the project should advocate for a formal Danish digital identity governance reform that separates fallback-path approval from primary-system operator control, or whether such a move is politically feasible within the 24-month timeframe.

responses.summary: Add 'conduct a formal technical working-group meeting with the MitID operator in month 3 of phase one to request access to platform-integrity APIs, app-distribution constraints, and contingency-planning documentation' to Incumbent Operator Positioning and Transparency Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: This project navigates a fundamental tension between institutional inertia and technical necessity. Existing platforms (Apple, Google) are entrenched through convenience, regulatory compliance pathways, and incumbent operator lock-in, yet Denmark's sovereign digital-identity strategy demands platform-neutral resilience. The selected levers target the decision-makers and mechanisms that can hardcode neutrality into procurement, standards, and governance *before* AltID locks in the same dependencies. Success hinges on moving from abstract sovereignty arguments to concrete technical evidence, institutional leverage points, and continuity-planning imperatives that make exclusion of fallback paths formally unjustifiable.

levers.name: Authority Engagement Sequencing and Escalation Pathway
levers.consequences: When and how you approach Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and relevant ministries shapes whether the project is treated as a credible policy input or dismissed as a fringe technical proposal. Early, informal engagement risks premature dismissal; delayed engagement reduces time for institutional response cycles and procurement influence. This lever determines whether institutional actors see platform neutrality as optional or as a compliance requirement they must defend or adopt.
options: Sequence engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen first as the primary procurement authority, followed by formal briefings to the MitID operator and relevant ministry working groups, with escalation to parliamentary committee members only if institutional response stalls after month 12.

Conduct parallel engagement tracks with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and academic/expert advisors simultaneously from month two onward, ensuring multiple institutional pressure points and reducing reliance on a single decision-maker's receptiveness.

Pursue engagement through sympathetic Folketinget committee members early (by month four), leveraging parliamentary pressure and oversight authority to signal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen that platform neutrality is a political expectation, not a technical nicety.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between early institutional visibility and the risk of antagonizing incumbent operators before technical credibility is established. The options overlook the timing costs: too-early escalation may trigger defensive coordination among incumbents, while too-late engagement may miss the formal AltID planning window where procurement language is actually written.

levers.name: Demonstrator Realism and Technical Ambition Boundary
levers.consequences: Setting the scope of the non-production demonstrators determines whether the project produces credible technical evidence or oversells unproven concepts. A lean, focused demonstrator (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS against a simulated identity provider) is defensible and achieves the goal of proving feasibility without feature creep. A more ambitious demonstrator (hardware tokens, multiple fallback paths, cross-platform support) risks cost overruns, timeline delays, and external skepticism about whether the project is serious about non-production constraints.
options: Build a single high-fidelity mobile demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS that authenticates against a realistic simulated Danish public-sector OpenID Connect environment, with security review by an external third party to certify the approach is sound.

Develop two parallel demonstrators with equal scope: the mobile pathway and a hardware-token-based fallback that operates entirely outside the app-store model, accepting increased timeline pressure and engineering complexity to show multiple technically credible alternatives.

Construct a minimal proof-of-concept mobile demonstrator (weeks 9–12) followed by a browser-based or cross-platform fallback prototype (weeks 13–16) that deliberately trades polish and feature completeness for speed, with the understanding that both are explicitly unfinished reference implementations.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between technical credibility and project timeline/budget constraints. None of the options adequately address how to manage external security review timelines or what happens if review findings require demonstrator redesign mid-project.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Communication and Transparency Posture
levers.consequences: How the project communicates with and about the MitID operator—and whether it frames MitID as an enemy, a legacy system, or a peer stakeholder—shapes the operator's willingness to cooperate, remain neutral, or actively oppose the work. Transparent engagement with the operator about the project's intent can yield data-sharing, security feedback, or at least non-interference; adversarial framing risks triggering institutional defensiveness and closed-door coordination with platform vendors. The posture also affects external credibility: perceived fair dealing improves trust with other public-sector stakeholders.
options: Engage the MitID operator as a peer stakeholder and domain expert from month two onward, inviting formal feedback on the feasibility report and demonstrator architecture, explicitly framing the project as complementary resilience planning rather than a critique of MitID's current design choices.

Maintain principled distance from the MitID operator while conducting technical research independently, publishing findings without pre-coordination, and allowing the operator to respond publicly to the project's proposals, preserving analytical integrity at the cost of potential operator defensiveness.

Request formal non-disclosure and data-sharing agreements with the MitID operator early in phase one, granting confidential access to platform-dependency details and trust-model documentation on condition that findings are reviewed by the operator before publication, trading speed and autonomy for deeper technical insight.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between collaborative legitimacy and analytical independence. The options do not account for the operator's likely institutional incentive to delay or minimize platform-dependency findings, or for the political risk that the operator uses confidentiality agreements to suppress inconvenient results.

levers.name: EU Standards Forum Timing and Contribution Strategy
levers.consequences: Timing entry into European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIW) working groups and standards forums determines whether the project influences upstream design or arrives too late to shape consensus. Early involvement (month 8–12) allows the project to seed platform-neutrality thinking into European design discussions before national implementations harden. Late involvement (month 16+) reduces leverage but may improve the credibility of the project's proposals if demonstrators and Danish procurement pressure have already built institutional momentum. This lever shapes whether the project's impact is primarily Danish or contributes to broader European resilience architecture.
options: Participate in relevant EDIW technical working groups and eIDAS regulatory forums from month eight onward, positioning the Danish project as an early proof of feasibility and contributing demonstrator findings directly to European interoperability and portability discussions as they emerge.

Defer substantive EU engagement until phase three (month 17+), allowing the project to mature demonstrators and secure domestic political traction first, then approach European standards bodies from a position of proven Danish institutional and technical credibility.

Identify and support individual technical experts and researchers from partner institutions to represent platform-neutral perspectives in existing European working groups throughout the project timeline, maintaining low institutional visibility while seeding ideas through decentralized advocacy.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between early influence and premature commitment of limited resources to EU forums that may not yield regulatory change. The options overlook the reality that European standards development operates on multiyear cycles and that late-stage contributions have minimal impact on already-drafted specifications.

levers.name: Fallback Path Certification and Governance Anchor
levers.consequences: Choosing which regulatory or governance framework—eIDAS Level Substantial, NSIS, Danish procurement standards, or a hybrid—should formally certify or mandate the fallback path determines who controls the requirement and how defensible it is against incumbent resistance. Anchoring to eIDAS creates European legal standing but may be slow and subject to member-state negotiation. Anchoring to Danish procurement policy is faster and more directly actionable but lacks the weight of EU regulation. A hybrid approach increases legitimacy but dilutes clear accountability. This lever determines whether the fallback requirement becomes a legal mandate or remains a best-practice recommendation.
options: Anchor the platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirement explicitly to eIDAS Level Substantial compliance criteria and the Digital Services Act's supplier-concentration concerns, framing it as a European interoperability obligation that Denmark must implement in procurement and AltID design.

Pursue inclusion of platform-neutrality language in Danish digital-identity procurement guidelines and the government's 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty strategy, operating entirely within national regulatory authority and avoiding the slower consensus-building of EU standards bodies.

Develop a hybrid governance model combining a non-binding Danish government recommendation for fallback mechanisms with a formal proposal to embed platform-neutrality expectations in the NSIS (Next-Generation Digital Identities) framework, creating both national and EU-level pressure points.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between regulatory scope and speed of implementation. Anchoring too heavily to EU processes delays Danish action; anchoring only nationally may be overturned in future administrations or dismissed as parochial. The options do not adequately address how to prevent incumbent operators from exploiting ambiguity between national and EU requirements to delay or water down platform-neutrality obligations.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Advocacy Amplification
levers.consequences: The composition of the coalition—whether it remains primarily academic and civil-society focused, incorporates sympathetic public-sector voices, or adds international security experts and foreign-government observers—determines the project's reach and credibility across different audiences. A pure academic coalition is credible to policymakers but lacks the institutional authority of public-sector insiders. Adding government sympathizers strengthens procurement arguments but risks accusations of regulatory capture. Adding international experts elevates the profile but introduces coordination complexity and potential geopolitical framing risks. This lever shapes which stakeholder groups the project can credibly influence and at what political cost.
options: Maintain a focused coalition of Danish academics, privacy advocates, security researchers, and civil-society organizations, deliberately avoiding formal government employment or positions, ensuring independence and public-interest credibility while accepting constraints on institutional access.

Recruit sympathetic technical staff and advisors from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the Danish Agency for Cybersecurity, and relevant university-affiliated government advisory bodies, leveraging insider expertise and procurement knowledge while managing perception of institutional bias and conflicts of interest.

Establish a formal international advisory board including digital-identity and security experts from allied nations, the European Commission's digital-identity unit, and relevant standards-development organizations, positioning the project as a European resilience initiative rather than a national Danish concern.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between credibility, institutional leverage, and political independence. The options overlook the practical risk that recruiting current government staff creates employment and career-advancement complications, and that international advisors introduce coordination delays and diffuse accountability for outcomes.

levers.name: Phase-Three Pivot Criteria and De-Scoping Trigger
levers.consequences: Defining in advance what constitutes insufficient AltID traction by month 16 (and what the phase-three fallback should be) prevents the project from drifting into unjustifiable efforts to force procurement change that is politically unavailable. If AltID procurement momentum is weak, the project should pivot to publication, coalition consolidation, and EU standards input. If AltID engagement is credible, the project can pursue formal procurement inclusion. This lever shapes the project's exit strategy and prevents sunk-cost thinking from extending work beyond realistic impact zones.
options: Establish a formal go/no-go gate at month 16 that evaluates whether Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has explicitly acknowledged the platform-neutrality proposal in writing and included fallback-access language in at least one published AltID design document; if not met, the project de-scopes phase three and pivots to publication and EU-standards contribution.

Define success by month 16 as securing a formal commitment (or non-binding letter of intent) from the AltID program governance body to conduct a formal procurement analysis on platform-neutral architectures; if not secured, shift phase-three resources entirely to external publication, data sharing, and coalition-managed advocacy.

Set the phase-three trigger as the achievement of substantive technical demonstrator completion and external security review by month 14, which then enables month 15–16 procurement outreach; if demonstrators are delayed beyond month 14, truncate phase three and reallocate budget to extended publication and dissemination timelines.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between optimistic institutional change and realistic political outcomes. The options overlook the fact that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may acknowledge the proposal without formally committing to procurement change, creating an ambiguous situation where the project cannot clearly invoke the pivot clause without appearing to give up prematurely.

responses.summary: Add 'convene a monthly advisory panel of sympathetic Folketinget committee members, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen staff, and external security experts to jointly review demonstrator findings and co-author procurement language recommendations' to Authority Engagement Sequencing and Escalation Pathway.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's digital identity infrastructure faces a structural supplier-concentration risk: MitID's trust model depends on Apple and Google platform signals, while the 2026–2029 public-sector strategy calls for reduced foreign-technology dependence. The project's core tension is between the institutional inertia of incumbent operators (who benefit from status quo) and the legitimate resilience and sovereignty demands of public administration. The levers below target this tension by reframing the issue from ideology to continuity risk, building demonstrable technical evidence, and embedding platform-neutral requirements into procurement and EU standards before the AltID ecosystem hardens. Success requires credible technical proof, institutional coalition pressure, and timing intervention at critical decision gates—not disruption for its own sake.

levers.name: Risk Reframing and Continuity Language
levers.consequences: Repositioning the project from a sovereignty or ideology argument to a supplier-concentration and business-continuity risk narrative changes which stakeholders view the issue as urgent and relevant. Continuity-planning language resonates with procurement teams, risk officers, and government auditors, while sovereignty framing triggers ideological pushback from technology optimists and incumbent vendors. The shift also influences which government bodies take ownership and which budget lines fund mitigation.
options: Frame all project materials, reports, and stakeholder pitches around supply-chain resilience, single-point-of-failure risk, and contingency-planning obligations rather than privacy or ideological independence, ensuring the argument aligns with government procurement and risk governance language.

Develop a formal business-continuity scenario document showing the operational impact and citizen service disruption if Apple or Google policies, integrity APIs, or distribution terms change, then present this to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's enterprise risk and contingency planning teams.

Engage the Danish Council for Cybersecurity and critical infrastructure protection bodies to formally designate digital identity as an essential-service dependency requiring platform-neutral fallback plans and regulatory resilience standards.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances reframing power against the risk of appearing to overstate threat severity. The core tension is between credibility (must feel proportionate to incumbent operators) and political traction (must feel urgent enough to move procurement). The options overlook how to sustain momentum if risk framing alone does not trigger formal procurement action within the phase-two window.

levers.name: Demonstrator Architecture and Audience Design
levers.consequences: The choice of which authentication standards, platforms, and fallback pathways to implement in the phase-two demonstrators directly shapes what stakeholders believe is technically feasible and how governments interpret the evidence. A demonstrator on GrapheneOS targeting security experts sends a different signal than a browser-based fallback targeting citizens; a FIDO2/OpenID Connect implementation is less threatening to MitID than a full biometric-alternative stack. The demonstrator's technical choices become the 'proof of concept' narrative that shapes whether procurement teams believe platform-neutral design is realistic or a research fantasy.
options: Build two complementary demonstrators—one mobile-oriented on GrapheneOS using FIDO2 and OpenID Connect for institutional credibility, and one browser-based fallback using hardware tokens or SMS-backed authentication—each explicitly scoped to avoid feature-parity competition with MitID while proving interoperability standards work.

Implement a single, high-fidelity mobile demonstrator on standard Android (not GrapheneOS) that mimics real-world MitID integration points and procurement expectations, then use it to negotiate a formal technical sandbox partnership with the MitID operator to prove coexistence rather than replacement.

Design a demonstrator that integrates with existing government identity-provider infrastructure (simulation of OIOSAML or similar) and publicly certifies the implementation against eIDAS and NSIS requirements, making the evidence legally and politically usable in procurement language.
levers.review_lever: This lever controls what 'proof' means to different audiences, but the options conflict on audience priority: security experts vs. procurement teams vs. operators. None of the options address how to prevent demonstrators from being dismissed as 'not production-ready' by stakeholders who benefit from the status quo, a critical credibility risk in phase three.

levers.name: EU Standards Coordination and Venue Selection
levers.consequences: Digital identity standards in Europe are set by multiple bodies (eIDAS committees, NSIS working groups, ETSI, ISO), and Denmark's advocacy has outsized influence if coordinated with other member states and aligned with EU Digital Identity Wallet rollout timelines. Engaging at the EU level extends the project's reach beyond Danish procurement but risks diluting focus and imposing longer timelines; conversely, ignoring EU coordination means missing the window when interoperability and fallback-path language is still negotiable in EU frameworks. The choice of which venue to prioritize and which coalition partners to activate determines whether platform-neutrality language becomes EU-wide or remains a Danish edge case.
options: Identify and join existing EU working groups (NSIS task forces, eIDAS technical groups) where platform-neutral and fallback-pathway language is already under discussion, then contribute the project's demonstrators and technical evidence as input to formal EU standards development, ensuring Danish advocacy is embedded in official EU processes.

Organize a discrete EU convening (workshop or technical symposium) in Copenhagen with member-state digital-identity leads, standardization bodies, and privacy advocates to build a coalition around resilience and reduced platform lock-in, then formalize this coalition's input into eIDAS wallet and NSIS requirements.

Engage the European Commission's digital-sovereignty and strategic-autonomy initiatives (such as the Digital Europe Programme or relevant Directorate-General units) to position platform-neutral digital identity as a European resilience requirement, then use that framing to shape EU procurement language and technical specifications.
levers.review_lever: This lever expands influence but creates timing and coordination complexity. The core tension is between exporting Denmark's message (broader impact, longer timelines) and protecting phase-three impact on Danish AltID (faster, narrower scope). The options assume EU bodies are receptive to this framing; none address how to proceed if platform neutrality is rejected or deprioritized at the EU level, leaving Denmark isolated.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Coexistence Model
levers.consequences: How the project approaches the MitID operator—as a hostile incumbent to circumvent or as a potential coexistence partner—determines whether phase-three procurement language is written against their resistance or negotiated with their implicit support. A cooperative model reduces hostility but risks co-optation or watering down fallback requirements; an adversarial model accelerates procurement pressure but hardens MitID's institutional defenses and may trigger legal or political countermeasures. The choice also affects whether phase-two technical work uses live MitID sandboxes (legitimizing but constraining) or simulated environments (faster but less credible to procurement).
options: Propose a formal partnership with MitID (Nets or Digital sikkerhed) where the project's fallback and platform-neutral work is explicitly framed as a continuity-planning service complementing MitID rather than replacing it, offering them a role in defining coexistence protocols and reducing their perceived threat.

Maintain technical and public independence from MitID while building a parallel coalition of Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, public-sector CIOs, and procurement officers who pressure MitID to accept platform-neutral and fallback-capable interoperability requirements without requiring the operator's voluntary cooperation.

Engage MitID in a limited, asymmetric dialogue where phase-two demonstrators are reviewed by MitID security teams for technical soundness without committing to formal partnership, then use their technical feedback (even if reluctant) as evidence of feasibility in phase-three procurement arguments.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between speed and institutional buy-in. The core weakness is that none of the options address what happens if MitID actively litigates or escalates to their board or shareholders to block procurement language that threatens their monopoly, a realistic political and legal risk in a phase-three window.

levers.name: AltID Engagement Timing and Procurement-Leverage Window
levers.consequences: AltID's rollout timeline and procurement-decision gates are fixed; the project must insert its requirements at the correct moment or miss the window entirely. If the project engages too early (phase one), AltID's technical architecture is not yet finalized and procurement language is premature; if too late (phase three month 20+), key architectural decisions are locked and platform-neutral requirements become expensive post-hoc additions. The timing lever determines whether the project is a credible voice in active procurement conversations or a marginal stakeholder offering feedback on decisions already made. Early success depends on identifying exactly when AltID's procurement language is drafted and who controls that document.
options: Embed a project representative or contracted advisor directly into AltID's technical and procurement working groups starting in phase two, ensuring real-time input into architecture discussions and early inclusion of platform-neutral and fallback-pathway language before formal requirements are finalized.

Map AltID's official procurement timeline and decision gates, then schedule demonstrator delivery, policy proposals, and coalition advocacy to align precisely with AltID's request-for-proposal drafting and stakeholder consultation windows, maximizing pressure at moments when requirements are still negotiable.

Initiate a formal written request from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen (secured in phase one) asking AltID to incorporate platform-neutral and fallback-pathway requirements into its procurement scope, establishing the requirement as an official mandate rather than optional input from an external research project.
levers.review_lever: This lever controls timing precision, but the options assume predictable AltID governance and openness to external input. The core weakness is that AltID may have already closed its procurement windows or may resist external pressure as threatening its timeline and budget, leaving the project unable to influence procurement even with credible technical evidence.

levers.name: Fallback Certification Framework and Authority Anchor
levers.consequences: A fallback authentication pathway requires formal legal or regulatory definition and approval; the project must decide whether to seek explicit government certification under eIDAS or NSIS, pursue implicit compliance through general procurement language, or establish a private-sector consortium standard. Formal government certification carries credibility and enforceability but requires regulatory bandwidth and may trigger years of approval cycles; implicit compliance is faster but leaves the fallback path vulnerable to future reinterpretation or withdrawal. The choice affects whether the fallback is a resilience guarantee or an optional policy preference with no enforcement mechanism.
options: Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the Ministry of Finance to establish a formal regulatory approval pathway for platform-neutral fallback authentication under eIDAS Substantial or NSIS Level 2, embedding the requirement into legal identity-infrastructure regulations rather than leaving it as procurement guidance.

Develop a voluntary industry certification scheme (possibly through a Danish security or standards body) that certifies fallback-authentication implementations as meeting resilience and interoperability criteria, creating market credibility without requiring government approval but also without enforcement power.

Anchor the fallback requirement in AltID's procurement contract terms and service-level agreements as a mandatory technical specification, making platform-neutral access a supplier obligation enforceable through contract penalties rather than a government regulation.
levers.review_lever: This lever determines enforceability but faces a core tension between speed and permanence. Formal regulatory approval is durable but slow; procurement contracts are fast but vulnerable to renegotiation or non-compliance. None of the options address how to prevent a future government from deprioritizing the fallback requirement once the initial political pressure fades.

levers.name: Coalition Credibility and External-Stakeholder Validation
levers.consequences: The coalition assembled in phase one shapes whether the project's message is heard as urgent legitimate concern or as fringe advocacy. A coalition spanning civil-society privacy advocates, academic security researchers, government risk officers, and sympathetic Folketinget members carries more weight than a coalition of technology idealists; conversely, including government-adjacent stakeholders risks accusations of regulatory capture, while excluding them leaves the project without institutional credibility. External validation—security audits, academic publications, expert endorsements—provides credibility but also creates delays and introduces uncertainty (reviewers may criticize the approach). The project must choose which stakeholders to court and in what sequence.
options: Build a coalition deliberately weighted toward government-adjacent stakeholders (Ministry of Finance risk officers, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen architects, Danish business continuity councils) and secure their formal endorsement of the resilience framing before public advocacy, ensuring procurement officers view the project as aligned with government risk strategy.

Publish independent academic and security-expert validation (peer-reviewed conference papers on the technical feasibility and threat analysis, third-party security reviews) before phase-two demonstrators are complete, establishing intellectual credibility that makes the project harder to dismiss as activist or ideological.

Engage civil-society and privacy-advocate organizations as leading voices in public communication and coalition governance, positioning the project as a democratic accountability initiative rather than a technical exercise, then sequentially add government and academic stakeholders to broaden perceived legitimacy.
levers.review_lever: This lever controls whose opinion matters to decision-makers, but the options reflect different implicit assumptions about whether Danish procurement is responsive to academic evidence, government risk assessments, or public pressure. The core weakness is that none of the options address how to manage conflict or public disagreement within the coalition if, for example, civil-society groups demand stronger platform-neutrality language while government partners prefer cautious, incremental steps.

responses.summary: Add 'engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's enterprise risk and critical-infrastructure teams to formally designate digital identity as an essential service requiring platform-neutral fallback plans' to Risk Reframing and Continuity Language.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope and Trust Model
levers.consequences: A narrow, standards-based demonstrator (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS plus browser-based fallback) delivers credible evidence that platform-neutral authentication is technically feasible without requiring retrofits to MitID or claiming production readiness. This evidence becomes the foundation for procurement language and standards proposals in phases two and three. However, demonstrators perceived as toy implementations or oversimplified will be dismissed by incumbent operators and procurement officers; the project must balance lean scope against sufficient architectural depth to withstand expert skepticism.
options: Build a single integrated mobile-plus-browser demonstrator using OpenID Connect and FIDO2 against a full mock Danish identity-provider stack, with explicit trust-path documentation and threat modeling to support procurement arguments

Develop two independent demonstrators—one mobile-focused on GrapheneOS with platform-integrity alternatives clearly named, one browser-token-based—and publish a side-by-side trust model comparison that allows procurement to choose preferred fallback patterns

Create a minimal hardware-token-plus-web demonstrator that deliberately excludes mobile OS entirely, framing it as the most radical but lowest-risk fallback path for continuity planning and positioning the other options as incremental improvements
levers.review: This lever governs the credibility-versus-scope trade-off, but the options underestimate the institutional cost of demonstrator governance. None address how the project will prevent the demonstrator from becoming a product roadmap pressure point or a target for incumbent veto based on 'incomplete security analysis.' Procurement officers will demand formal security review; the options do not allocate budget or timeline for external validation.

levers.name: Procurement Language and AltID Engagement
levers.consequences: Securing explicit platform-neutrality, fallback access, or supplier-concentration-risk language in official AltID design documents or procurement terms would lock in resilience requirements before architectural decisions harden. Success depends on formal institutional traction with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the AltID operator by mid-project. However, incumbent operators and platform vendors will resist language perceived as anti-commercial or technically prescriptive; weak institutional relationships or late-stage engagement will leave phase three with no realistic channel for procurement influence.
options: Target Digitaliseringsstyrelsen directly with a formal fallback-access requirement proposal in phase one, position the demonstrators as compliance evidence in phase three, and prepare a public procurement template that procurement officers can adopt without citing the project by name

Engage the MitID operator and AltID program leadership incrementally throughout phases one and two via technical working groups and security reviews, building consensus on resilience language before formal procurement starts, rather than attempting to inject requirements retroactively in phase three

Coordinate with the European Commission's eIDAS2 implementation task force to align fallback-access and platform-neutrality requirements at the EU level first, then use EU framework obligations to pressure Danish procurement to adopt parallel language, reducing reliance on bilateral Danish negotiation
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between direct Danish procurement influence and indirect EU-level leverage, but the options overlook timing risk. If AltID procurement accelerates faster than the project's EU engagement matures, Danish intervention becomes obsolete. None of the options provide a clear contingency if institutional relationships fail to develop or if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen deprioritizes the resilience framing in favor of user-convenience or cost arguments.

levers.name: Coalition Structure and Advocacy Framing
levers.consequences: Building a coalition spanning civil-society, academic, security, and parliamentary stakeholders increases political cover and channels for influence beyond formal procurement channels. Framing the issue as resilience and supplier concentration rather than ideology or privacy maximizes institutional receptiveness. However, coalition members often have divergent interests—privacy advocates may prioritize data minimization over fallback access, security experts may prioritize certification complexity over platform neutrality—and managing coalition messaging without alienating members is difficult; an internally fractured coalition can damage credibility more than no coalition at all.
options: Build a tight technical-policy coalition of 3–5 core institutions (Danish cybersecurity authority, parliament's digital committee representatives, one major university) with written joint position papers, avoiding broader civil-society partners whose agendas may conflict or dilute the resilience message

Construct a broad multi-stakeholder coalition (civil society, business, academic, parliamentary) with explicitly segmented messaging—resilience and procurement for government stakeholders, privacy and sovereignty for civil society, interoperability and risk mitigation for business and security experts—and maintain a public unified statement on supplier concentration risk

Prioritize parliamentary engagement via Folketinget's digital policy committee as the primary coalition anchor, treating other stakeholders as supporting voices rather than equal partners, and frame resilience requirements as a non-partisan continuity-planning obligation that should not require civil-society consensus
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between coalition breadth and message coherence, but the options overlook the risk of captured messaging. If coalition partners include industry players or operators with commercial stakes in the status quo, coalition credibility becomes hostage to accusations of conflicts of interest. None of the options address how the project will maintain independence if coalition members demand editorial control over key deliverables or public statements.

levers.name: Standards Venue and EU Alignment
levers.consequences: Engaging with EU-level technical working groups and the European Digital Identity Wallet framework allows the project to amplify impact beyond Denmark and frame requirements as EU-wide interoperability concerns rather than national parochialism. Success here creates external pressure on Danish procurement to comply with EU standards. However, EU processes are slow and crowded; meaningful influence requires sustained engagement and technical expertise on standards bodies that the project may lack, and EU-level language may be weaker or more generic than Danish-specific procurement requirements.
options: Focus the project's EU engagement narrowly on the eIDAS2 technical working group responsible for wallet architecture, targeting specific fallback-access or platform-neutrality language in the implementing technical specifications, with phase three dedicated to translating EU language into Danish procurement terms

Position the project as a Dutch-Danish bilateral initiative aligned with broader European digital sovereignty concerns, leveraging existing EU policy conversations around GAIA-X and digital autonomy to make platform-neutral identity a European strategic priority rather than a single-nation issue

Maintain EU engagement as a secondary, publication-focused effort rather than a primary advocacy channel; publish findings in open EU forums and standards consortia, allowing policy influence to propagate organically through networks rather than attempting to secure formal language commitments from EU institutions
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between EU-level leverage and Danish autonomy in procurement, but the options overlook the risk of misalignment. If EU standards evolve toward mandatory platform-neutral approaches, Denmark may be forced to overhaul AltID sooner than optimal; conversely, if EU action stalls, Danish advocacy becomes trapped in multilateral gridlock. None of the options address what the project does if EU bodies reject platform-neutrality language as anti-competitive or commercially hostile.

levers.name: Phase-Gate Funding Conditions and Risk Mitigation
levers.consequences: Conditional phase-gate funding (phase two and three dependent on phase one deliverables and institutional traction) protects budget against institutional failure but creates cash-flow and staffing continuity challenges if phase-gate conditions are not met or take longer to satisfy than anticipated. Clear phase-gate criteria increase accountability but may create pressure to manufacture deliverables that meet criteria on paper without genuine institutional engagement. Conversely, vague conditions allow flexibility but invite scope creep and budget hemorrhage.
options: Define phase-gate criteria tightly: phase two requires a published feasibility report, one formal meeting with a named authority, and a project financing plan; phase three requires documented institutional interest from AltID program leadership or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, or a documented contingency pivot toward publication and EU standards input instead of procurement targeting

Use phase gates as flexibility points rather than hard stops; define phase one as unconditional and phase two as conditional on 'meaningful institutional engagement' (defined as written correspondence or meeting documentation, not formal approval), allowing the project to continue phase two if phase-one outreach begins but has not yet yielded formal responses

Split phases two and three budget into demonstrator development (unconditional) and procurement intervention (conditional), so that technical work proceeds regardless of institutional traction, but procurement and standards effort is deferred or truncated if phase-one institutional engagement is weak
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between accountability and flexibility, but the options underestimate the political cost of visibly failing phase gates. If phase-one institutional engagement is weak and the project pivots to 'publication and EU standards input,' stakeholders may view the project as having failed its core mission regardless of technical quality. None of the options provide a clear mechanism for recovering credibility if early institutional outreach is rebuffed or if procurement timelines shift faster than the project can respond.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Positioning and Transparency Strategy
levers.consequences: The project does not attempt to build a production replacement for MitID or explicitly attack the MitID operator, but the project's framing of platform-vendor dependence as a risk necessarily implies that the operator's current trust model is problematic. The operator may interpret the project as a threat and attempt to block access to technical documentation, obstruct stakeholder relationships, or lobby against procurement changes. Transparency about the project's goals and technical assumptions can reduce adversarial escalation, but full transparency may accelerate incumbent opposition. Stealth or non-engagement invites accusations of bad faith if discovered later.
options: Approach the MitID operator early in phase one with a transparent but diplomatically framed proposal for a technical working relationship, positioning the project as supporting MitID's resilience and contingency preparedness rather than as a competitive threat, and requesting access to technical documentation and operator perspectives on fallback-access architecture

Treat the MitID operator as a stakeholder for consultation but not as a coalition partner; brief operator leadership on the project scope and findings but do not request approval or ongoing collaboration, maintaining independence from operator consent while avoiding public rhetoric that frames MitID's current model as fundamentally deficient

Conduct the project without formal outreach to the MitID operator until phase-three procurement intervention; document any operator objections or blocking attempts as evidence of incumbent resistance, and use public disclosure of barriers to platform-neutral requirements as a policy argument in favor of regulatory intervention
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between transparency and strategic autonomy, but the options overlook the asymmetry of information power. The MitID operator knows its own architecture and constraints; the project does not. Early operator engagement risks providing veto leverage to the incumbent, but non-engagement risks building demonstrators or proposals that the operator can quickly dismiss as technically infeasible. None of the options address how the project will verify operator claims about technical constraints or funding discontinuities if the operator's interests diverge from the project's goals.

levers.name: Fallback-Path Certification and Governance Model
levers.consequences: Any platform-neutral or fallback authentication path will eventually require formal certification, assurance review, or regulatory approval to be trusted in production MitID or successor contexts. The project's phase three must clarify whether fallback paths are certified within the existing MitID governance framework (conservative, slow, and operator-controlled) or within eIDAS2/NSIS frameworks (newer, less operator-dependent, but also less mature and with longer EU consensus timelines). Choosing the wrong governance path defers implementation indefinitely or locks fallback access into a framework that itself depends on platform vendors or foreign regulation.
options: Propose that fallback-access certification be integrated into the eIDAS2 Level Substantial assurance framework as a required 'resilience attestation,' allowing fallback paths to be certified independently of MitID governance and creating a clean separation between primary and backup identity paths

Develop a lightweight self-certification or voluntary-assurance model for fallback paths that does not require formal MitID-operator approval or EU-level certification, allowing demonstrators to be deployed in pilot or non-critical contexts without regulatory gatekeeping, and using real-world pilot data to inform formal certification requirements later

Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to establish a contingency-path certification fast-track within Danish digital identity governance, creating a parallel certification process for fallback authentication that is explicitly faster and lighter than MitID certification but still maintains sufficient assurance to meet eIDAS2 substantial-level requirements
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between certification rigor and deployment speed, but the options obscure a fundamental governance risk. If fallback paths are certified independently of MitID, they may not interoperate with production MitID systems in a crisis; if they are certified within MitID governance, the MitID operator retains veto power over fallback requirements. None of the options address whether the project should advocate for a formal Danish digital identity governance reform that separates fallback-path approval from primary-system operator control, or whether such a move is politically feasible within the 24-month timeframe.

levers.name: Authority Engagement Sequencing and Escalation Pathway
levers.consequences: When and how you approach Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and relevant ministries shapes whether the project is treated as a credible policy input or dismissed as a fringe technical proposal. Early, informal engagement risks premature dismissal; delayed engagement reduces time for institutional response cycles and procurement influence. This lever determines whether institutional actors see platform neutrality as optional or as a compliance requirement they must defend or adopt.
options: Sequence engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen first as the primary procurement authority, followed by formal briefings to the MitID operator and relevant ministry working groups, with escalation to parliamentary committee members only if institutional response stalls after month 12.

Conduct parallel engagement tracks with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and academic/expert advisors simultaneously from month two onward, ensuring multiple institutional pressure points and reducing reliance on a single decision-maker's receptiveness.

Pursue engagement through sympathetic Folketinget committee members early (by month four), leveraging parliamentary pressure and oversight authority to signal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen that platform neutrality is a political expectation, not a technical nicety.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between early institutional visibility and the risk of antagonizing incumbent operators before technical credibility is established. The options overlook the timing costs: too-early escalation may trigger defensive coordination among incumbents, while too-late engagement may miss the formal AltID planning window where procurement language is actually written.

levers.name: Demonstrator Realism and Technical Ambition Boundary
levers.consequences: Setting the scope of the non-production demonstrators determines whether the project produces credible technical evidence or oversells unproven concepts. A lean, focused demonstrator (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS against a simulated identity provider) is defensible and achieves the goal of proving feasibility without feature creep. A more ambitious demonstrator (hardware tokens, multiple fallback paths, cross-platform support) risks cost overruns, timeline delays, and external skepticism about whether the project is serious about non-production constraints.
options: Build a single high-fidelity mobile demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS that authenticates against a realistic simulated Danish public-sector OpenID Connect environment, with security review by an external third party to certify the approach is sound.

Develop two parallel demonstrators with equal scope: the mobile pathway and a hardware-token-based fallback that operates entirely outside the app-store model, accepting increased timeline pressure and engineering complexity to show multiple technically credible alternatives.

Construct a minimal proof-of-concept mobile demonstrator (weeks 9–12) followed by a browser-based or cross-platform fallback prototype (weeks 13–16) that deliberately trades polish and feature completeness for speed, with the understanding that both are explicitly unfinished reference implementations.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between technical credibility and project timeline/budget constraints. None of the options adequately address how to manage external security review timelines or what happens if review findings require demonstrator redesign mid-project.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Communication and Transparency Posture
levers.consequences: How the project communicates with and about the MitID operator—and whether it frames MitID as an enemy, a legacy system, or a peer stakeholder—shapes the operator's willingness to cooperate, remain neutral, or actively oppose the work. Transparent engagement with the operator about the project's intent can yield data-sharing, security feedback, or at least non-interference; adversarial framing risks triggering institutional defensiveness and closed-door coordination with platform vendors. The posture also affects external credibility: perceived fair dealing improves trust with other public-sector stakeholders.
options: Engage the MitID operator as a peer stakeholder and domain expert from month two onward, inviting formal feedback on the feasibility report and demonstrator architecture, explicitly framing the project as complementary resilience planning rather than a critique of MitID's current design choices.

Maintain principled distance from the MitID operator while conducting technical research independently, publishing findings without pre-coordination, and allowing the operator to respond publicly to the project's proposals, preserving analytical integrity at the cost of potential operator defensiveness.

Request formal non-disclosure and data-sharing agreements with the MitID operator early in phase one, granting confidential access to platform-dependency details and trust-model documentation on condition that findings are reviewed by the operator before publication, trading speed and autonomy for deeper technical insight.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between collaborative legitimacy and analytical independence. The options do not account for the operator's likely institutional incentive to delay or minimize platform-dependency findings, or for the political risk that the operator uses confidentiality agreements to suppress inconvenient results.

levers.name: EU Standards Forum Timing and Contribution Strategy
levers.consequences: Timing entry into European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIW) working groups and standards forums determines whether the project influences upstream design or arrives too late to shape consensus. Early involvement (month 8–12) allows the project to seed platform-neutrality thinking into European design discussions before national implementations harden. Late involvement (month 16+) reduces leverage but may improve the credibility of the project's proposals if demonstrators and Danish procurement pressure have already built institutional momentum. This lever shapes whether the project's impact is primarily Danish or contributes to broader European resilience architecture.
options: Participate in relevant EDIW technical working groups and eIDAS regulatory forums from month eight onward, positioning the Danish project as an early proof of feasibility and contributing demonstrator findings directly to European interoperability and portability discussions as they emerge.

Defer substantive EU engagement until phase three (month 17+), allowing the project to mature demonstrators and secure domestic political traction first, then approach European standards bodies from a position of proven Danish institutional and technical credibility.

Identify and support individual technical experts and researchers from partner institutions to represent platform-neutral perspectives in existing European working groups throughout the project timeline, maintaining low institutional visibility while seeding ideas through decentralized advocacy.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between early influence and premature commitment of limited resources to EU forums that may not yield regulatory change. The options overlook the reality that European standards development operates on multiyear cycles and that late-stage contributions have minimal impact on already-drafted specifications.

levers.name: Fallback Path Certification and Governance Anchor
levers.consequences: Choosing which regulatory or governance framework—eIDAS Level Substantial, NSIS, Danish procurement standards, or a hybrid—should formally certify or mandate the fallback path determines who controls the requirement and how defensible it is against incumbent resistance. Anchoring to eIDAS creates European legal standing but may be slow and subject to member-state negotiation. Anchoring to Danish procurement policy is faster and more directly actionable but lacks the weight of EU regulation. A hybrid approach increases legitimacy but dilutes clear accountability. This lever determines whether the fallback requirement becomes a legal mandate or remains a best-practice recommendation.
options: Anchor the platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirement explicitly to eIDAS Level Substantial compliance criteria and the Digital Services Act's supplier-concentration concerns, framing it as a European interoperability obligation that Denmark must implement in procurement and AltID design.

Pursue inclusion of platform-neutrality language in Danish digital-identity procurement guidelines and the government's 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty strategy, operating entirely within national regulatory authority and avoiding the slower consensus-building of EU standards bodies.

Develop a hybrid governance model combining a non-binding Danish government recommendation for fallback mechanisms with a formal proposal to embed platform-neutrality expectations in the NSIS (Next-Generation Digital Identities) framework, creating both national and EU-level pressure points.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between regulatory scope and speed of implementation. Anchoring too heavily to EU processes delays Danish action; anchoring only nationally may be overturned in future administrations or dismissed as parochial. The options do not adequately address how to prevent incumbent operators from exploiting ambiguity between national and EU requirements to delay or water down platform-neutrality obligations.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Advocacy Amplification
levers.consequences: The composition of the coalition—whether it remains primarily academic and civil-society focused, incorporates sympathetic public-sector voices, or adds international security experts and foreign-government observers—determines the project's reach and credibility across different audiences. A pure academic coalition is credible to policymakers but lacks the institutional authority of public-sector insiders. Adding government sympathizers strengthens procurement arguments but risks accusations of regulatory capture. Adding international experts elevates the profile but introduces coordination complexity and potential geopolitical framing risks. This lever shapes which stakeholder groups the project can credibly influence and at what political cost.
options: Maintain a focused coalition of Danish academics, privacy advocates, security researchers, and civil-society organizations, deliberately avoiding formal government employment or positions, ensuring independence and public-interest credibility while accepting constraints on institutional access.

Recruit sympathetic technical staff and advisors from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the Danish Agency for Cybersecurity, and relevant university-affiliated government advisory bodies, leveraging insider expertise and procurement knowledge while managing perception of institutional bias and conflicts of interest.

Establish a formal international advisory board including digital-identity and security experts from allied nations, the European Commission's digital-identity unit, and relevant standards-development organizations, positioning the project as a European resilience initiative rather than a national Danish concern.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between credibility, institutional leverage, and political independence. The options overlook the practical risk that recruiting current government staff creates employment and career-advancement complications, and that international advisors introduce coordination delays and diffuse accountability for outcomes.

levers.name: Phase-Three Pivot Criteria and De-Scoping Trigger
levers.consequences: Defining in advance what constitutes insufficient AltID traction by month 16 (and what the phase-three fallback should be) prevents the project from drifting into unjustifiable efforts to force procurement change that is politically unavailable. If AltID procurement momentum is weak, the project should pivot to publication, coalition consolidation, and EU standards input. If AltID engagement is credible, the project can pursue formal procurement inclusion. This lever shapes the project's exit strategy and prevents sunk-cost thinking from extending work beyond realistic impact zones.
options: Establish a formal go/no-go gate at month 16 that evaluates whether Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has explicitly acknowledged the platform-neutrality proposal in writing and included fallback-access language in at least one published AltID design document; if not met, the project de-scopes phase three and pivots to publication and EU-standards contribution.

Define success by month 16 as securing a formal commitment (or non-binding letter of intent) from the AltID program governance body to conduct a formal procurement analysis on platform-neutral architectures; if not secured, shift phase-three resources entirely to external publication, data sharing, and coalition-managed advocacy.

Set the phase-three trigger as the achievement of substantive technical demonstrator completion and external security review by month 14, which then enables month 15–16 procurement outreach; if demonstrators are delayed beyond month 14, truncate phase three and reallocate budget to extended publication and dissemination timelines.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between optimistic institutional change and realistic political outcomes. The options overlook the fact that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may acknowledge the proposal without formally committing to procurement change, creating an ambiguous situation where the project cannot clearly invoke the pivot clause without appearing to give up prematurely.

levers.name: Risk Reframing and Continuity Language
levers.consequences: Repositioning the project from a sovereignty or ideology argument to a supplier-concentration and business-continuity risk narrative changes which stakeholders view the issue as urgent and relevant. Continuity-planning language resonates with procurement teams, risk officers, and government auditors, while sovereignty framing triggers ideological pushback from technology optimists and incumbent vendors. The shift also influences which government bodies take ownership and which budget lines fund mitigation.
options: Frame all project materials, reports, and stakeholder pitches around supply-chain resilience, single-point-of-failure risk, and contingency-planning obligations rather than privacy or ideological independence, ensuring the argument aligns with government procurement and risk governance language.

Develop a formal business-continuity scenario document showing the operational impact and citizen service disruption if Apple or Google policies, integrity APIs, or distribution terms change, then present this to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's enterprise risk and contingency planning teams.

Engage the Danish Council for Cybersecurity and critical infrastructure protection bodies to formally designate digital identity as an essential-service dependency requiring platform-neutral fallback plans and regulatory resilience standards.
levers.review: This lever balances reframing power against the risk of appearing to overstate threat severity. The core tension is between credibility (must feel proportionate to incumbent operators) and political traction (must feel urgent enough to move procurement). The options overlook how to sustain momentum if risk framing alone does not trigger formal procurement action within the phase-two window.

levers.name: Demonstrator Architecture and Audience Design
levers.consequences: The choice of which authentication standards, platforms, and fallback pathways to implement in the phase-two demonstrators directly shapes what stakeholders believe is technically feasible and how governments interpret the evidence. A demonstrator on GrapheneOS targeting security experts sends a different signal than a browser-based fallback targeting citizens; a FIDO2/OpenID Connect implementation is less threatening to MitID than a full biometric-alternative stack.
