The demonstrator's technical choices become the 'proof of concept' narrative that shapes whether procurement teams believe platform-neutral design is realistic or a research fantasy.
options: Build two complementary demonstrators—one mobile-oriented on GrapheneOS using FIDO2 and OpenID Connect for institutional credibility, and one browser-based fallback using hardware tokens or SMS-backed authentication—each explicitly scoped to avoid feature-parity competition with MitID while proving interoperability standards work.

Implement a single, high-fidelity mobile demonstrator on standard Android (not GrapheneOS) that mimics real-world MitID integration points and procurement expectations, then use it to negotiate a formal technical sandbox partnership with the MitID operator to prove coexistence rather than replacement.

Design a demonstrator that integrates with existing government identity-provider infrastructure (simulation of OIOSAML or similar) and publicly certifies the implementation against eIDAS and NSIS requirements, making the evidence legally and politically usable in procurement language.
levers.review: This lever controls what 'proof' means to different audiences, but the options conflict on audience priority: security experts vs. procurement teams vs. operators. None of the options address how to prevent demonstrators from being dismissed as 'not production-ready' by stakeholders who benefit from the status quo, a critical credibility risk in phase three.

levers.name: EU Standards Coordination and Venue Selection
levers.consequences: Digital identity standards in Europe are set by multiple bodies (eIDAS committees, NSIS working groups, ETSI, ISO), and Denmark's advocacy has outsized influence if coordinated with other member states and aligned with EU Digital Identity Wallet rollout timelines. Engaging at the EU level extends the project's reach beyond Danish procurement but risks diluting focus and imposing longer timelines; conversely, ignoring EU coordination means missing the window when interoperability and fallback-path language is still negotiable in EU frameworks. The choice of which venue to prioritize and which coalition partners to activate determines whether platform-neutrality language becomes EU-wide or remains a Danish edge case.
options: Identify and join existing EU working groups (NSIS task forces, eIDAS technical groups) where platform-neutral and fallback-pathway language is already under discussion, then contribute the project's demonstrators and technical evidence as input to formal EU standards development, ensuring Danish advocacy is embedded in official EU processes.

Organize a discrete EU convening (workshop or technical symposium) in Copenhagen with member-state digital-identity leads, standardization bodies, and privacy advocates to build a coalition around resilience and reduced platform lock-in, then formalize this coalition's input into eIDAS wallet and NSIS requirements.

Engage the European Commission's digital-sovereignty and strategic-autonomy initiatives (such as the Digital Europe Programme or relevant Directorate-General units) to position platform-neutral digital identity as a European resilience requirement, then use that framing to shape EU procurement language and technical specifications.
levers.review: This lever expands influence but creates timing and coordination complexity. The core tension is between exporting Denmark's message (broader impact, longer timelines) and protecting phase-three impact on Danish AltID (faster, narrower scope). The options assume EU bodies are receptive to this framing; none address how to proceed if platform neutrality is rejected or deprioritized at the EU level, leaving Denmark isolated.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Coexistence Model
levers.consequences: How the project approaches the MitID operator—as a hostile incumbent to circumvent or as a potential coexistence partner—determines whether phase-three procurement language is written against their resistance or negotiated with their implicit support. A cooperative model reduces hostility but risks co-optation or watering down fallback requirements; an adversarial model accelerates procurement pressure but hardens MitID's institutional defenses and may trigger legal or political countermeasures. The choice also affects whether phase-two technical work uses live MitID sandboxes (legitimizing but constraining) or simulated environments (faster but less credible to procurement).
options: Propose a formal partnership with MitID (Nets or Digital sikkerhed) where the project's fallback and platform-neutral work is explicitly framed as a continuity-planning service complementing MitID rather than replacing it, offering them a role in defining coexistence protocols and reducing their perceived threat.

Maintain technical and public independence from MitID while building a parallel coalition of Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, public-sector CIOs, and procurement officers who pressure MitID to accept platform-neutral and fallback-capable interoperability requirements without requiring the operator's voluntary cooperation.

Engage MitID in a limited, asymmetric dialogue where phase-two demonstrators are reviewed by MitID security teams for technical soundness without committing to formal partnership, then use their technical feedback (even if reluctant) as evidence of feasibility in phase-three procurement arguments.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between speed and institutional buy-in. The core weakness is that none of the options address what happens if MitID actively litigates or escalates to their board or shareholders to block procurement language that threatens their monopoly, a realistic political and legal risk in a phase-three window.

levers.name: AltID Engagement Timing and Procurement-Leverage Window
levers.consequences: AltID's rollout timeline and procurement-decision gates are fixed; the project must insert its requirements at the correct moment or miss the window entirely. If the project engages too early (phase one), AltID's technical architecture is not yet finalized and procurement language is premature; if too late (phase three month 20+), key architectural decisions are locked and platform-neutral requirements become expensive post-hoc additions. The timing lever determines whether the project is a credible voice in active procurement conversations or a marginal stakeholder offering feedback on decisions already made. Early success depends on identifying exactly when AltID's procurement language is drafted and who controls that document.
options: Embed a project representative or contracted advisor directly into AltID's technical and procurement working groups starting in phase two, ensuring real-time input into architecture discussions and early inclusion of platform-neutral and fallback-pathway language before formal requirements are finalized.

Map AltID's official procurement timeline and decision gates, then schedule demonstrator delivery, policy proposals, and coalition advocacy to align precisely with AltID's request-for-proposal drafting and stakeholder consultation windows, maximizing pressure at moments when requirements are still negotiable.

Initiate a formal written request from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen (secured in phase one) asking AltID to incorporate platform-neutral and fallback-pathway requirements into its procurement scope, establishing the requirement as an official mandate rather than optional input from an external research project.
levers.review: This lever controls timing precision, but the options assume predictable AltID governance and openness to external input. The core weakness is that AltID may have already closed its procurement windows or may resist external pressure as threatening its timeline and budget, leaving the project unable to influence procurement even with credible technical evidence.

levers.name: Fallback Certification Framework and Authority Anchor
levers.consequences: A fallback authentication pathway requires formal legal or regulatory definition and approval; the project must decide whether to seek explicit government certification under eIDAS or NSIS, pursue implicit compliance through general procurement language, or establish a private-sector consortium standard. Formal government certification carries credibility and enforceability but requires regulatory bandwidth and may trigger years of approval cycles; implicit compliance is faster but leaves the fallback path vulnerable to future reinterpretation or withdrawal. The choice affects whether the fallback is a resilience guarantee or an optional policy preference with no enforcement mechanism.
options: Work with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the Ministry of Finance to establish a formal regulatory approval pathway for platform-neutral fallback authentication under eIDAS Substantial or NSIS Level 2, embedding the requirement into legal identity-infrastructure regulations rather than leaving it as procurement guidance.

Develop a voluntary industry certification scheme (possibly through a Danish security or standards body) that certifies fallback-authentication implementations as meeting resilience and interoperability criteria, creating market credibility without requiring government approval but also without enforcement power.

Anchor the fallback requirement in AltID's procurement contract terms and service-level agreements as a mandatory technical specification, making platform-neutral access a supplier obligation enforceable through contract penalties rather than a government regulation.
levers.review: This lever determines enforceability but faces a core tension between speed and permanence. Formal regulatory approval is durable but slow; procurement contracts are fast but vulnerable to renegotiation or non-compliance. None of the options address how to prevent a future government from deprioritizing the fallback requirement once the initial political pressure fades.

levers.name: Coalition Credibility and External-Stakeholder Validation
levers.consequences: The coalition assembled in phase one shapes whether the project's message is heard as urgent legitimate concern or as fringe advocacy. A coalition spanning civil-society privacy advocates, academic security researchers, government risk officers, and sympathetic Folketinget members carries more weight than a coalition of technology idealists; conversely, including government-adjacent stakeholders risks accusations of regulatory capture, while excluding them leaves the project without institutional credibility. External validation—security audits, academic publications, expert endorsements—provides credibility but also creates delays and introduces uncertainty (reviewers may criticize the approach). The project must choose which stakeholders to court and in what sequence.
options: Build a coalition deliberately weighted toward government-adjacent stakeholders (Ministry of Finance risk officers, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen architects, Danish business continuity councils) and secure their formal endorsement of the resilience framing before public advocacy, ensuring procurement officers view the project as aligned with government risk strategy.

Publish independent academic and security-expert validation (peer-reviewed conference papers on the technical feasibility and threat analysis, third-party security reviews) before phase-two demonstrators are complete, establishing intellectual credibility that makes the project harder to dismiss as activist or ideological.

Engage civil-society and privacy-advocate organizations as leading voices in public communication and coalition governance, positioning the project as a democratic accountability initiative rather than a technical exercise, then sequentially add government and academic stakeholders to broaden perceived legitimacy.
levers.review: This lever controls whose opinion matters to decision-makers, but the options reflect different implicit assumptions about whether Danish procurement is responsive to academic evidence, government risk assessments, or public pressure. The core weakness is that none of the options address how to manage conflict or public disagreement within the coalition if, for example, civil-society groups demand stronger platform-neutrality language while government partners prefer cautious, incremental steps.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

It requires a physical base in Copenhagen for research, development, and stakeholder meetings. The project involves building demonstrators, engaging with government bodies, and influencing procurement processes, all of which necessitate physical presence and interaction. The project also requires a physical location with access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings.
